parties: Between Timothy Engen, Applicant, and Disciplinary Board of Kingston Penitentiary, in the person of Mr. Thomas Troughton, Respondent
citation: Federal Court Judgments: [1987] F.C.J. No. 641 Action No. T-487-87
court: Federal Court of Canada - Trial Division Ottawa, Ontario Denault, J.
body_html:
Heard: May 7, 1987
Judgment: July 24, 1987

Judicial review -- Penitentiary -- Disciplinary hearing -- Representation by counsel.

Mr. John S. Hill, for the Applicant.

Ms. Meg Kinnear, for the Respondent.

DENAULT, J. (Reasons for Order):-- The applicant is asking the Court to issue a writ of certiorari to quash his conviction by the Disciplinary Board of the Kingston Penitentiary for a disciplinary offence (s. 39(b) of the Penitentiary Service Regulations) [Footnote: C.R.C., c. 1251 (as amended), hereinafter referred to as "the Regulations".]

The question is whether the inmate, who was charged with an intermediary disciplinary offence, was entitled to representation by Counsel in the particular circumstances of the case at bar.

FACTS:

Since 1981 the applicant has been serving a nine-year term of imprisonment at Kingston.

On June 26, 1986 Mr Engen was charged under s 39(b) of the Regulations with having assaulted or threatened to assault another person, a correctional officer, an offence classified as intermediary. Section 38(8) of the Regulations provides that:

38. (8) An inmate who is found guilty of a disciplinary offence that is determined by the directives to be an intermediary misconduct is liable to one or more of the following punishments:

(a) a warning or reprimand;

(b) the loss of privileges;

(c) a fine of not more than $50 to be recovered in accordance with subsection (12);

(d) reimbursement of Her Majesty, in the manner established by the directives, up to a maximum of $500, for the amount of damages caused wilfully or negligently to

(i) any property of Her Majesty, or

(ii) the property of another person where Her Majesty has reimbursed such person for the amount of damages;

(e) subject to subsection (10) dissociation from other inmates for a period not exceeding thirty consecutive days.

It is worth making a brief review of the background to the regulatory amendment made on July 4, 1985 to s 38(2) to (4) of the Penitentiary Service Regulations. The old Regulations provided that an inmate convicted of a disciplinary offence could lose his privileges, except in the case of a flagrant or serious offence when in addition to his privileges he could lose his statutory remission, as well as being dissociated for a period not exceeding thirty days. In March 1985 the Federal Court of Appeal held in Howard [Footnote: Howard v. Stony Mountain Institution, [1984] 2 FC 642. On June 13, 1985 the Supreme Court of Canada granted a motion for leave to appeal. ] that an inmate charged with a serious disciplinary offence for which he risked losing earned remission was justified in being represented by counsel. Whether by chance or as a direct result of this judgment, the Regulations were amended on July 4, 1985. The new Regulations now create three classes of disciplinary offences:

Returning now to the circumstances of the case at bar, the applicant was formally notified on July 7, 1986 of the charges laid. That same day he wrote the Queen's Correctional Law Project to obtain the services of a lawyer.

On July 10, 1986 Mr Engen appeared before Mr Troughton, the respondent in this case. The applicant asked to be allowed representation by counsel during the disciplinary hearing. Mr Troughton adjourned the case and ordered the two parties to submit written arguments to him on the point of law as to whether the applicant should be represented by counsel before the Disciplinary Board.

The events which followed this first hearing are described as follows in the applicant's affidavit:

12. That on July 17, 1986, John Skinner, a Student Member of the Queen's Correctional Law Project delivered by hand a letter to the Respondent requesting that he be allowed to represent me at my disciplinary hearing . . . Exhibit "D" . . .

13. That on July 17, 1986 Mr Skinner appeared in front of the Respondent at the disciplinary court of Kingston Penitentiary. At that time, the Respondent instructed Mr Skinner to make detailed submissions to both him and the Warden of Kingston Penitentiary concerning the matter of representing me at my disciplinary hearing.

14. That Mr Skinner objected to any input from the institution determining the issue of counsel representing inmates at their disciplinary hearings. On July 22, 1986, Mr Skinner delivered, by hand, a letter explaining his position on this matter to both the Respondent and the Assistant Warden of Kingston Penitentiary, Mr F A Sissons. . . . Exhibit "E" . . .

15. That on August 5, 1986, Mr Richard Weatherston, Counsel for the Correctional Service of Canada, sent both the Respondent and the Queen's Correctional Law Project a letter indicating that it is their position that "Correctional Services Canada has the right to make submissions with respect to an inmate's request to have counsel present at a disciplinary hearing". . . . Exhibit "F" . . .

16. That on August 14, 1986, I appeared before the Respondent in the disciplinary court of Kingston Penitentiary. At that time, the Respondent made a ruling based on the submissions tendered by Mr Skinner and Mr Weatherston that the Correctional Services has [sic] the right to make submissions regarding an inmate's request to be represented by counsel. As the Correctional Officer who charged me was not available at this hearing, it was further remanded to a time when he could be present.

17. That on August 21, 1986, I once again appeared before the Respondent in the disciplinary court of Kingston Penitentiary. At that time, the Respondent once again considered the issue of whether the CSC has the right to make submissions on an inmate's entitlement to be represented by counsel on a disciplinary charge. The Respondent ruled that the Correctional Services do have such a right and that he would accept their submissions on this issue. He then remanded my hearing for another two weeks in order to allow me to appeal his ruling to the Federal Court of Canada, Trial Division if I so desired.

At a final hearing held on October 15, 1986 the charge laid against Mr Engen was tried. He was found guilty of the offence stated in s 39(b) of the Regulations. As a consequence of this conviction, the applicant was sentenced to pay a fine of $40 and to twenty-one days' punitive dissociation, which was "suspended" for a period of ninety days. That meant that if the applicant was not found guilty of committing another intermediary or flagrant offence within this ninety-day period, he would not have to serve the sentence of dissociation.

At the very outset of the hearing on October 15, 1986, the question of whether Mr Engen could be represented by counsel was argued. After a lengthy discussion of Howard v Stony Mountain Institution, supra, and Baile v Mission Institution Disciplinary Court, (1986), 6 FTR 69, the respondent concluded that in the circumstances of the case Mr Engen was not entitled to the services of counsel.

LAW:

It is now well established that a disciplinary board, though it is not a court, is still subject to a duty to act fairly [Footnote: Martineau v. Matsqui Disciplinary Committee, [1980] 1 SCR 602, at 637; Cardinal v. Director of Kent Institution, [1985] 2 SCR 643, at 653.]. If evidence is presented that this duty was not carried out, certiorari will be available to the injured party who is seeking relief [Footnote: Martineau, supra, at 629-30]. However, before granting an application for certiorari, a judge must ensure that the injustice committed was a serious one. As Pigeon J observed in Martineau (No 2) [Footnote: Supra, at 637; see also Cardinal, supra, at 654.]:

I must, however, stress that the order issued by Mahoney J deals only with the jurisdiction of the Trial Division, not with the actual availability of the relief in the circumstances of the case. This is subject to the exercise of judicial discretion and in this respect it will be essential that the requirements of prison discipline be borne in mind, just as it is essential that the requirements of the effective administration of criminal justice be borne in mind when dealing with applications for certiorari before trial, as pointed out in Attorney General of Quebec v Cohen ([1979] 2 SCR 305). It is specially important that the remedy be granted only in cases of serious injustice and that proper care be taken to prevent such proceedings from being used to delay deserved punishment so long that it is made ineffective, if not altogether avoided.

The Court must now, bearing in mind this note of caution sounded by the Supreme Court, consider the question raised by the case at bar: was Mr Engen entitled to be represented by counsel at his hearing before the Kingston Penitentiary Disciplinary Board?

Mr Engen based his argument on Howard, supra, Bailey, supra and Régis Tremblay v Presiding Officer of Disciplinary Tribunal of Laval Institution [Footnote: T-1440-86, Rouleau J, April 9, 1987, not reported. This case is now on appeal: A-270-87. ]. In the first of these cases, a writ of prohibition was sought following the refusal by the Presiding Officer of the Disciplinary Court to recognize the right of the accused to have his counsel present. The inmate in that case had been charged with an offence in the flagrant or serious category under the old Penitentiary Service Regulations. On appeal Thurlow CJ, after observing that s 7 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms conferred no absolute right to representation by counsel, proceeded to examine the rules for determining whether the right to counsel should be recognized in a given case. He said the following at 662-663 of his reasons:

I am of the opinion that the enactment of section 7 has not created any absolute right to counsel in all such proceedings. It is undoubtedly of the greatest importance to a person whose life, liberty or security of the person are at stake to have the opportunity to present his case as fully and adequately as possible. The advantages of having the assistance of counsel for that purpose are not in doubt. But what is required is an opportunity to present the case adequately and I do not think it can be affirmed that in no case can such an opportunity be afforded without also as part of it affording the right to representation by counsel at the hearing.

Once that position is reached it appears to me that whether or not the person has a right to representation by counsel will depend on the circumstances of the particular case, its nature, its gravity, its complexity, the capacity of the inmate himself to understand the case and present his defence. The list is not exhaustive. And from this, it seems to me, it follows that whether or not an inmate's request for representation by counsel can lawfully be refused is not properly referred to as a matter of discretion but is a matter of right where the circumstances are such that the opportunity to present the case adequately calls for representation by counsel. It may be that where the circumstances do not point to that conclusion a residual authority to permit counsel nevertheless is exercisable by the appropriate official but that area is not I think within the purview of section 7.

In short, the entire issue turns on whether, in light of the circumstances of the case at bar, an adequate presentation of Mr Engen's case required representation by counsel.

At p 664 of the judgment, Thurlow CJ explained the role of the Presiding Officer of the Court and defined this Court's power of intervention:

He [the Presiding Officer of the Court] will no doubt have to consider and take a position on whether the case is one in which the request for counsel can be denied. And he must be prepared to act on his view. But, in my opinion, his denial of such a request cannot be regarded as an adjudication of the right and cannot prevent a superior court in the exercise of supervisory jurisdiction from determining the question on its own.

The task now before this Court is to consider whether, in the case at bar, the circumstances were such as to require that counsel be present at the hearing of the case before the Disciplinary Board.

NATURE AND GRAVITY OF OFFENCE:

No violence was involved in the offence with which Mr Engen was charged in the case at bar. By its very nature a threat assumes that the person making it has not translated into action what he has described orally.

I should point out that the fact the offence with which Mr Engen is charged is one classified as intermediary does not automatically preclude his right to counsel. It is the actual gravity of the offence which must be considered, not the name attached to it. A contrary conclusion would allow the prison authorities to get around the nuisance of having counsel present by allowing them to classify as intermediary an offence which would previously have been regarded as flagrant.

Despite this, it is still true that the commission of an intermediary offence does not entail the severe consequences that result from commission of a flagrant offence. Only the latter may lead to the loss by the inmate of his right to statutory remission (s 38(9)(f)(i)) or his right to earned remission, acquired before July 1, 1978 (s 38(a)(f)(ii)).

In considering the gravity of the offence, the applicant argued that this Court has a duty to take into account the possible repercussions that a guilty verdict by a Disciplinary Board may have. He contended that these are of two kinds: (1) the conviction may influence the decision of the National Parole Board to grant the inmate parole subject to mandatory supervision: see s 15.4(4) of the Parole Act, RSC 1970 c P-2 (as amended); (2) such a decision may also cause the Earned Remission Committee to deny the applicant his earned remission. In the case at bar the Board notified the applicant on August 19, 1986 that any decision regarding his right to days of earned remission for June had been suspended until judgment was rendered in connection with the disciplinary offences. In support of this argument, the applicant cited Bailey, supra and Tremblay, supra.

Counsel for the respondent argued that the effect which a conviction by the Disciplinary Board may have on subsequent proceedings should not be considered by this Court in its review of the gravity of the offence. Counsel for the respondent stated her rebuttal as follows (Memorandum of Fact and Law, p 8):

Likewise, it is submitted that the possibility that a conviction for a disciplinary offence will have consequences for the Applicant with respect to other decisions affecting him ought not be a factor to be evaluated in the determination of the seriousness of the charge faced by the inmate. Such questions as parole or earned remission are separate administrative decisions and different factors are considered by the boards that make these decisions. Further, they are not relevant to the primary issue which is whether the inmate has adequate opportunity to present his case.

Miss Kinnear then referred the Court to the following passage from the reasons for judgment of Hugessen J in Knockaert v Commr of Corrections (1986), 55 CR (3d) 171, at 186-7:

In my view, this appeal should be decided, as it was in first instance, on the very narrow basis that the administrative decision to refuse to award earned remission to an inmate because of his misbehaviour does not constitute punishment for that misbehaviour. While it is obviously the case that the line between reward and punishment may sometimes become blurred, it seems to me that the statutory scheme of earned remission, as set out in s 24(1) of the Penitentiary Act and as defined and expanded in the commissioner's directive, clearly envisages a monthly assessment of each individual inmate's performance, and such assessment properly takes into account whether the inmate has conformed to institutional and other rules during that month. While breach of rules may bring punishment, compliance with them, along with consistent application of effort to the program, may also bring reward; though the two may be opposite sides of the same coin, they are nonetheless quite distinct.

I will discuss the effect of this decision on the case at bar shortly.

I do not dispute that the right recognized by the Court of Appeal in Howard consists essentially in enabling the inmate to present his case adequately. If the circumstances of the particular case, examined in light of the rules stated by Thurlow CJ, indicate that the presence of counsel is necessary for there to be an adequate presentation of the inmate's case, the latter will have an unchallengeable right to such representation. However, I would point out that the right derives from the guarantee made by s 7 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, which provides:

7. Everyone has the right to life, liberty and security of the person and the right not to be deprived thereof except in accordance with the principles of fundamental justice.

The liberty of an inmate must therefore be threatened in order to set in motion the machinery of this constitutional guarantee [Footnote: See Walker v. Kingston Penitentiary Disciplinary Board, (1986) 3 FTR 109, at 110.]. The more danger there is of an invasion of the individual's liberty, the greater will be the need for him to present his case adequately. It would be naive to suggest that the Court, in weighing the seriousness of the offence committed, should ignore the possible consequences of a disciplinary conviction in subsequent proceedings - proceedings which, I repeat, will in turn deal a severe blow to the already shaky structure of an inmate's "liberty". In this regard I refer with approval to the remarks of Rouleau J in Tremblay, supra, at 35-36:

So far as the penalty that could be imposed is concerned, it is true that the Presiding Judge of the Disciplinary Tribunal could not, under s 38(8) of the Regulations, sentence the applicant to the loss of his right to statutory or earned remission since the offence was in the intermediary category. However, like any other inmate the applicant risks not being granted days of remission by the Earned Remission Committee because of the charge laid against him. In this regard, I would refer the parties to the following comments of Prof [Michael] Jackson, who in his well-documented article "The Right to Counsel in Prison Disciplinary Hearings" [(1986) 20 UBCL Rev 221], at pp 279-80, writes that:

. . . While a prisoner cannot be sentenced at the disciplinary board hearing to loss of remission for an intermediary offence, he will nevertheless have his liberty interest affected by failing to earn remission arising directly from the commission of the offence as a result of the earned remission scheme. As I have explained, for every three days in punitive dissociation a prisoner will fail to earn one day of remission and, depending upon the gravity of the offence, the Earned Remission Board can fail to credit up to the maximum of fifteen days remission for that month. [My emphasis]

. . . . .

For these prisoners and indeed for all prisoners who inevitably fail to earn remission following conviction of an intermediary offence, to be told that they do not have the right to counsel because, at the disciplinary hearing, the independent chairperson could not sentence them to loss of remission, is calculated to do nothing but further prisoners' disdain for the Correctional Service's claim that justice now runs behind prison walls.

It can thus be concluded on this point that the penalty which could be imposed does not rest solely with the Presiding Judge of the Disciplinary Tribunal.

Reed J, in Savard v Edmonton Institution Disciplinary Court (Presiding Member) et al, [Footnote: (1986), 3FTR 1, at 5. Appeal dismissed on May 14, 1987, A-188-86.] also stressed the need to consider the potential consequences of a guilty verdict by the Disciplinary Court:

I interpret the Howard case as indicating that the particular circumstances of the individual case must be considered as well as the theoretical consequences which could flow from charges generally of the type in question.

The observations made by Hugessen J in Knockaert, supra, do not in any way conflict with the conclusions I have arrived at. In that case Hugessen J merely established that the refusal by an Earned Remission Committee to grant days of remission did not amount to "punishment" within the meaning of s 11(h) of the Charter. I do not dispute this conclusion in any way. However, while such a refusal may not constitute punishment it nevertheless has the direct consequence of invading the limited freedom enjoyed by an inmate. Any other conclusion would mean that, in a prison situation, liberty is a rather meaningless concept. In Knockaert, supra, the Federal Court of Appeal had to deal with implementation of s 11(h), not s 7, of the Charter. What is not a punishment may nevertheless be a deprivation of liberty.

I would however repeat that consideration of the gravity of an offence is only one of the aspects for analysis in determining whether an inmate has the right to representation by counsel. Since in the case at bar a guilty verdict by the Disciplinary Board could greatly influence the decisions made subsequently by the Earned Remission Committee and the National Parole Board, the other criteria stated by Thurlow CJ must be very strictly applied.

COMPLEXITY OF OFFENCE AND CAPACITY OF INMATE TO PRESENT HIS DEFENCE HIMSELF

The offence in the case at bar contains no legal traps into which the applicant could fall. The threatening words were admitted by Mr Engen. In defence, he sought to prove that there had been ". . . a misunderstanding as to the implication of th[e] statement", Transcript, p.20. As can be seen from the transcript of the hearing, Mr Engen is a very articulate individual. However, his ability to speak for himself did not suffice to persuade the Presiding Officer of the Board that what he had said was inoffensive.

Further, it was clear that Mr Engen was well prepared to face the charges against him. It was he who first raised the question of the effect which a guilty verdict could have on his chances of obtaining days of earned remission, and on the possibility of his being granted parole. It was also the applicant who drew the existence of Bailey, supra, to the attention of the Presiding Officer.

As appeared from the transcript, this was not Mr Engen's first appearance before the Disciplinary Board. He could therefore hardly argue that he was not familiar with the procedure followed. Finally, I should mention that Mr Engen had about four months in which to obtain information and prepare his case, which was more than reasonable.

After making a careful review of all the circumstances of the case at bar, I have come to the conclusion that despite the severe consequences which could result from Mr Engen's conviction, I cannot in light of the other criteria stated by Thurlow CJ conclude that the applicant was unable to present his case adequately without counsel. I therefore consider that there was no breach of s 7 of the Charter.

Though it is not necessary in dealing with the case at bar, I should like to make a slight digression. Counsel for the respondent placed great emphasis on the fact that this case raised no complex questions of law. I would agree that in the case at bar, in view of the applicant's obvious ability to present his defence himself, he was fully competent to discuss questions of fact which were at issue. However, the fact that a case raises only questions of fact does not always mean that counsel is not necessary. As Prof Jackson said, supra, at pp 251-2 of his article:

Given that a prison disciplinary court's decision frequently turns on disputed questions of fact, most often reduced to the institutional witness's word against that of the prisoner, the need for skilled cross-examination to reveal mistakes of identity, faulty perceptions or cloudy memory becomes a critical issue. The protection afforded by cross-examination is meaningless if it is not exercised by someone with the skill and training to comprehend and maximize its utility. Further, the stigma of suspicion which often plagues a prisoner when rebutting incriminating evidence can be displaced by the simple fact that a lawyer is conducting the questioning.
. . . . .
The delineation of material facts is as much a product of advocacy as the presentation of legal argument. Given the educational level of many prisoners it is unrealistic to expect them to effectively engage in such a process. The absence of counsel can frustrate the prisoner's opportunity to present a full answer and defence, transposing the doctrine of fairness to a purely mythical plane.

It will suffice to recall the words of Lord Denning in Pett v Greyhound Racing Association Ltd, [1968] 2 All ER 545, at 549 (CA):

It is not every man who has the ability to defend himself on his own. He cannot bring out the points in his own favour or the weaknesses in the other side. He may be tongue-tied or nervous, confused or wanting in intelligence. He cannot examine or cross-examine witnesses. We see it every day. A magistrate says to a man: "You can ask any questions you like"; whereupon the man immediately starts to make a speech. If justice is to be done, he ought to have the help of someone to speak for him; and who better than a lawyer who has been trained for the task? I should have thought, therefore, that when a man's reputation or livelihood is at stake, he not only has a right to speak by his own mouth, he also has a right to speak by counsel or solicitor.

From these two quotations it can be seen that one of the essential points to be considered in deciding whether a right to counsel exists is whether a person is able to defend himself. I realize that in Howard, supra, at 688 MacGuigan J made the following comment:

I disapprove particularly of the suggestion in the Tarrant case [R v Secretary of State, Ex parte Tarrant, [1984] 1 All ER 799] that one of the relevant considerations should be the capacity of a particular prisoner to present his own case. With respect, no presiding officer could be in a position, at the outset of disciplinary proceedings, to make a summary judgment of such a kind before a prisoner had been heard by him.

First, I should note that Thurlow CJ and Pratte J did not share the opinion of their brother judge on this point. With respect, I think that there can be many situations in which such a decision might be rendered by the Presiding Officer of the tribunal. For example, an inmate who is well-known to be easily irritated or slightly maladjusted would not be in a position to present his case calmly and concisely. As another example, if the charge laid was the result of an altercation between an inmate and a guard, it is very likely that in cross-examining the latter a prisoner would very soon be in danger of shifting from his forced politeness to a flood of invectives. In the two examples mentioned, the presence of a lawyer would be highly desirable. Furthermore, in practice an inmate will probably appear before the same Presiding Officer several times during his period of imprisonment. In such circumstances, a Presiding Officer would be all the more able to determine whether the inmate is capable of representing himself. In the case at bar, the respective paths of Mr Engen and the respondent had crossed many times in the past.

The purpose of this brief comment is simply to emphasize the importance that must be attached to considering whether an inmate is able to present his defence himself. In the case at bar, as I mentioned above, there is no doubt that the applicant was highly qualified to do so.

Before concluding, I must analyse the other argument put forward by counsel for the applicant: he argued that in authorizing the Correctional Service of Canada to file written arguments regarding an inmate's right to be represented by counsel, the respondent had illegally delegated his powers. He further contended that this action had the effect of vitiating the final decision of the Presiding Officer by a presumption of partiality.

I cannot agree with these arguments. First, the duty imposed on the Presiding Officer of a tribunal is to act fairly. This means that both parties must be given the right to be heard: both the one who strikes and the one who tries to ward off the blow. The presumption of partiality against the respondent would be much greater if he had attended only to the arguments of the applicant. Second, there would have been an illegal delegation of powers if the Presiding Officer of the tribunal had made someone else responsible for determining whether the applicant was entitled to the services of counsel. In the case at bar, after weighing the arguments submitted by each party, Mr Troughton concluded that the applicant was not entitled to representation by counsel. In so far as he took the final decision himself, I see no infringement of a right.

For all these reasons, I consider that a writ of certiorari should not be issued.

DENAULT, J.
